summary: I am Joseph Anthony Grande, born in Italy on July 8th, 1898, the oldest of eight siblings. My family brought me to New Jersey in the early 1900s during a wave of Italian immigration to the United States. I worked at the Aluminum Company of America, striving to achieve the American Dream alongside my wife Theresa Cometot and our six children. Despite setbacks like the failed aluminum can patent, I left a legacy as a devoted father, businessman, and Catholic leader until my death in 1962.

Italy is where I was born and where my family’s story begins. My mother, Rosa Dinunzi, was very young when she had me, and I was the only one of my siblings born there. We left Italy in the early 1900s to escape poverty and poor living conditions, seeking a better life in America. Italy’s culture and heritage remained important to us even after immigrating, especially through our Catholic faith and connections like visits to the Vatican.

New Jersey became my home after my parents brought me here as a child in the early 1900s. It was where I grew up during the Americanization Movement and later built a life with my wife Theresa and our children. We lived comfortably in a nice house and I worked at the Aluminum Company of America nearby. New Jersey was also the setting for my sons’ scrap metal business after World War II, which was tied to the Italian Mafia and caused frequent government scrutiny.

The United States was the land of opportunity that my family sought when we left Italy in the early 1900s. We were part of the massive wave of over four million Italian immigrants who came here seeking a better life. Growing up here during the Americanization Movement shaped my career and family life. Despite challenges, including economic setbacks and involvement with organized crime through my sons, the U.S. was where we pursued and partially achieved the American Dream.

As Italian Americans, my family and I were part of the fifth largest ethnic group in the United States. We maintained strong cultural ties through our Catholic faith and community, even as we adapted to American customs during the Americanization Movement. Our story reflects both the struggles and successes common among Italian Americans, balancing hard work, family, and sometimes complicated relationships with organized crime.

I grew up during the Americanization Movement, which aimed to educate and assimilate immigrants like me into American customs and policies. This movement had a significant impact on my career, lifestyle, and family life in New Jersey. It shaped how I and many others adapted to life in the United States while still holding onto our Italian heritage.

Theresa Cometot was my wife, whom I met in the 1920s. She was born in Pennsylvania to parents from Calabria, Italy, and like me, part of the Italian immigrant community. Theresa worked at the Ford Motor Company while I worked at the Aluminum Company of America. Together, we raised five sons and one daughter, building a comfortable life in New Jersey and pursuing the American Dream.

Pennsylvania is where my wife Theresa Cometot was born, though her parents were originally from Calabria, Italy. It was part of the larger Italian immigrant story in the United States during the early 20th century. Theresa later moved to New Jersey where we built our family and life together.

Theresa worked at the Ford Motor Company during the 1930s and 1940s, earning around $925 a year by 1945. It was a stable job that helped support our family while I worked at the Aluminum Company of America. Ford was part of the industrial landscape that shaped our lives as Italian immigrants in America.

I worked at the Aluminum Company of America, now known as Alcoa, one of the largest aluminum companies in the world. By 1935, I was part of an elite crew there and even tried to create a patent for the aluminum can, though it ultimately fell through. This company was central to my career and later indirectly connected to my sons’ scrap metal business after World War II.

I drove a blue Packard, which was a symbol of the success Theresa and I had achieved by 1935. The Packard was a luxury car that represented our comfortable life in New Jersey after years of hard work. It was part of the American Dream we were striving for as Italian immigrants.

All five of my sons served in World War II, which was a defining event for our family. After the war, they started a scrap metal business that was somewhat tied to the Italian Mafia. The war changed many things for us, including the opportunities and challenges my sons faced in post-war America.

After World War II, my sons Robert and Paul became involved in a scrap metal business that had ties to the Italian Mafia. They would steal aluminum shipments from the Aluminum Company of America in New Jersey and resell the metal, leading to frequent government raids. Despite the risks, they had a network that warned them in advance, allowing them to hide the aluminum and continue their operations for some time.

My great Uncle Paul was a devout Catholic who frequently visited the Vatican in Rome. He had the honor of singing for the pope during his visits, a testament to our family's strong Catholic faith. When he returned to New Jersey, he would sing sacred songs like 'Ave Maria' for our family, keeping our religious and cultural heritage alive.

'Ave Maria' is a song my great Uncle Paul often sang for our family after returning from the Vatican. It symbolizes our family's deep Catholic roots and connection to our Italian heritage. These songs remind us of the faith and traditions that sustained us through our challenging journey in America.
